sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: A vaccine study measured antibody titers against a viral surface protein using ELISA. Participants received either placebo, a low-dose vaccine, or a high-dose vaccine. Four weeks later, mean ELISA absorbance and neutralization activity were measured. The high-dose group showed the greatest absorbance and neutralization. However, some participants with high absorbance had only modest neutralization, suggesting that not all antibodies measured by ELISA blocked viral entry.
questions.stem: Which group showed the greatest neutralization activity?

explanations.D: Correct. The high-dose vaccine group has the highest neutralization value at 76%, exceeding the low-dose and placebo groups.
explanations.A: The placebo group has minimal neutralization.
explanations.B: The low-dose group improves neutralization but not as much as the high-dose group.
explanations.C: The bars are clearly not equal.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. ELISA absorbance and viral neutralization activity four weeks after placebo or vaccination.

questions.stem: What does ELISA absorbance primarily measure in this experiment?

choices.C: Antibody binding to the viral surface protein

explanations.C: Correct. ELISA absorbance reflects how much participant antibody binds the immobilized viral protein, producing a detectable signal.
explanations.A: T-cell receptor rearrangement is not measured by this ELISA.
explanations.B: ELISA binding does not directly measure viral replication.
explanations.D: ATP synthesis is unrelated to the antibody-binding assay.

questions.stem: Why might high ELISA absorbance not always match high neutralization?

choices.B: Some antibodies bind the antigen without blocking viral entry
choices.D: Absorbance measures only red blood cell volume

explanations.B: Correct. Binding antibodies can recognize antigen sites that do not interfere with receptor binding or fusion. Thus ELISA can detect non-neutralizing antibodies.
explanations.A: The assay does not necessarily destroy neutralizing antibodies.
explanations.C: Neutralization generally requires relevant antigen binding.
explanations.D: Absorbance here reflects antibody binding, not RBC volume.

questions.stem: Which immune cell is primarily responsible for secreting antibodies?

choices.A: Plasma cells derived from B cells

explanations.A: Correct. Activated B cells can differentiate into plasma cells that secrete antibodies.
explanations.B: Cytotoxic T cells kill infected cells but do not secrete antibodies.
explanations.C: Erythrocytes transport oxygen and lack nuclei in humans.
explanations.D: Neutrophils and platelets are distinct cell types and are not antibody-secreting plasma cells.

questions.stem: Which follow-up would best distinguish binding from neutralizing antibody responses?

choices.D: Compare ELISA titers with an assay measuring prevention of viral entry into target cells
choices.A: Measure only total protein in serum
choices.B: Repeat ELISA with no antigen coated on the plate
choices.C: Count the number of mitochondria in lymphocytes

explanations.D: Correct. A viral-entry or neutralization assay directly tests functional blocking activity, allowing comparison with binding titers from ELISA.
explanations.B: No-antigen ELISA would not measure antigen-specific binding.
explanations.C: Mitochondrial counts do not distinguish antibody function.

questions.passage: A microbiology class tested an antibiotic that inhibits bacterial ribosomes. Cultures were treated at time 0 with no drug, a low dose, or a high dose. Optical density increased rapidly in the untreated culture, increased slowly at low dose, and remained nearly flat at high dose. When the high-dose culture was plated after 6 hours, some colonies still formed after drug removal. The instructor described the high-dose condition as bacteriostatic under these conditions rather than fully bactericidal.
questions.stem: Which condition shows the fastest bacterial growth?

explanations.C: Correct. The no-drug curve rises steeply to the highest optical density, indicating the fastest growth.
explanations.A: Low dose slows growth relative to no drug.
explanations.B: High dose is nearly flat and shows minimal growth.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Optical density of bacterial cultures treated with no drug, low-dose ribosome inhibitor, or high-dose ribosome inhibitor.

questions.stem: What cellular process is directly targeted by a ribosome inhibitor?

explanations.B: Correct. Ribosomes synthesize proteins by translating mRNA. A ribosome inhibitor directly impairs translation.
explanations.A: DNA replication uses polymerases, not ribosomes.
explanations.C: Transcription factor phosphorylation is a signaling event, not the ribosome function.
explanations.D: Beta-oxidation occurs in metabolism and is not directly performed by ribosomes.

questions.stem: Why is the high-dose condition described as bacteriostatic rather than fully bactericidal?

choices.A: Some bacteria formed colonies after the drug was removed
choices.B: Optical density increased fastest at high dose
choices.C: The drug directly lysed all cells immediately
choices.D: Bacteriostatic means the drug mutates host DNA

explanations.A: Correct. Bacteriostatic drugs inhibit growth without necessarily killing every cell. Colony formation after removal indicates that some bacteria survived.
explanations.B: The high-dose optical density was nearly flat, not fastest.
explanations.C: Immediate lysis of all cells would be bactericidal and would not yield colonies.
explanations.D: Bacteriostatic refers to growth inhibition, not host DNA mutation.

questions.stem: Which observation best supports dose dependence?

choices.D: Growth is fastest with no drug, slower with low dose, and nearly absent with high dose
choices.A: All cultures reach the same optical density
choices.C: The low-dose and high-dose curves overlap perfectly

explanations.D: Correct. The graded response across no drug, low dose, and high dose shows increasing inhibition with increasing drug concentration.
explanations.A: The cultures do not reach the same optical density.
explanations.C: The low-dose and high-dose curves are distinct.

questions.stem: Which cellular outcome would be most immediate after ribosome inhibition?

choices.C: Reduced synthesis of new bacterial proteins
choices.A: Immediate degradation of all existing DNA
choices.D: Complete disappearance of the plasma membrane

explanations.C: Correct. Blocking ribosomes prevents new protein synthesis, which slows growth because proteins needed for division and metabolism cannot be made efficiently.
explanations.A: Existing DNA is not immediately degraded by ribosome inhibition.
explanations.B: Translation would decrease rather than increase.
explanations.D: The plasma membrane does not instantly disappear as a direct ribosome-inhibition effect.
